run.result:
The mechanism is a **flatten-on-save / collect-on-read** pattern. Here's the complete data path.

## The model field

`EntityEdge` declares a catch-all dict for anything beyond the fixed schema (`graphiti_core/edges.py:209-211`):

```python
attributes: dict[str, Any] = Field(
    default={}, description='Additional attributes of the edge. Dependent on edge name'
)
```

## Saving — flatten attributes into the top-level property map

In `EntityEdge.save()` (`edges.py:236-257`), the fixed fields are assembled into `edge_data`, then the custom attributes are merged in as siblings — not nested:

```python
edge_data: dict[str, Any] = { 'source_uuid': ..., 'uuid': ..., 'fact': ..., ... }
edge_data.update(self.attributes or {})   # line 252 — flattens attrs to top level

await driver.execute_query(ENTITY_EDGE_SAVE, edge_data=edge_data)
```

The query then writes the **entire** dict as the relationship's properties with `SET r = $edge_data` (`models/edges/edge_db_queries.py:33-39`):

```cypher
MERGE (source)-[r:RELATES_TO {uuid: $uuid}]->(target)
SET r = $edge_data
```

Because `SET r = $edge_data` replaces all properties with whatever keys are in the map, each custom attribute becomes its own first-class property on the `RELATES_TO` relationship. (`SET r =` also means a re-save with fewer attributes drops the old ones — it's a full overwrite, not a merge.)

## Retrieving — grab all properties, then subtract the known ones

The return clause doesn't enumerate custom keys (it can't — they vary per edge name). Instead it pulls the whole property map via `properties(e)` (`edges.py:40-54`):

```cypher
RETURN
    e.uuid AS uuid,
    ...
    properties(e) AS attributes
```

So `attributes` initially contains *everything*, including the fixed fields. `get_entity_edge_from_record()` (`edges.py:453-481`) reconstructs the typed fields from their dedicated record keys, then **pops every known field out of `attributes`** so only the genuinely-custom keys remain:

```python
edge = EntityEdge(..., attributes=record['attributes'])

edge.attributes.pop('uuid', None)
edge.attributes.pop('source_node_uuid', None)
edge.attributes.pop('fact', None)
edge.attributes.pop('name', None)
# ... fact_embedding, group_id, episodes, created_at, expired_at, valid_at, invalid_at
```

## Summary

| Phase | Mechanism | Location |
|---|---|---|
| Save | `edge_data.update(self.attributes)` flattens custom attrs to top level | `edges.py:252` |
| Save | `SET r = $edge_data` writes all keys as relationship properties | `edge_db_queries.py:37` |
| Read | `properties(e) AS attributes` returns the full property map | `edges.py:53` |
| Read | `.pop()` the known fields so only custom attrs remain | `edges.py:469-479` |

The design avoids hardcoding attribute names anywhere: custom attributes ride along as ordinary graph properties, and the round-trip cleanly separates them from the typed schema fields. One note — the bulk path (`ENTITY_EDGE_SAVE_BULK`, `edge_db_queries.py:41-49`) relies on the same `SET r = edge` flattening, so edges built for bulk save must already have their attributes merged into the edge dict the same way.

answer:
The mechanism is a **flatten-on-save / collect-on-read** pattern. Here's the complete data path.

## The model field

`EntityEdge` declares a catch-all dict for anything beyond the fixed schema (`graphiti_core/edges.py:209-211`):

```python
attributes: dict[str, Any] = Field(
    default={}, description='Additional attributes of the edge. Dependent on edge name'
)
```

## Saving — flatten attributes into the top-level property map

In `EntityEdge.save()` (`edges.py:236-257`), the fixed fields are assembled into `edge_data`, then the custom attributes are merged in as siblings — not nested:

```python
edge_data: dict[str, Any] = { 'source_uuid': ..., 'uuid': ..., 'fact': ..., ... }
edge_data.update(self.attributes or {})   # line 252 — flattens attrs to top level

await driver.execute_query(ENTITY_EDGE_SAVE, edge_data=edge_data)
```

The query then writes the **entire** dict as the relationship's properties with `SET r = $edge_data` (`models/edges/edge_db_queries.py:33-39`):

```cypher

SET r = $edge_data
```

Because `SET r = $edge_data` replaces all properties with whatever keys are in the map, each custom attribute becomes its own first-class property on the `RELATES_TO` relationship. (`SET r =` also means a re-save with fewer attributes drops the old ones — it's a full overwrite, not a merge.)

## Retrieving — grab all properties, then subtract the known ones

The return clause doesn't enumerate custom keys (it can't — they vary per edge name). Instead it pulls the whole property map via `properties(e)` (`edges.py:40-54`):

```cypher
RETURN
    e.uuid AS uuid,
    ...
    properties(e) AS attributes
```

So `attributes` initially contains *everything*, including the fixed fields. `get_entity_edge_from_record()` (`edges.py:453-481`) reconstructs the typed fields from their dedicated record keys, then **pops every known field out of `attributes`** so only the genuinely-custom keys remain:

```python
edge = EntityEdge(..., attributes=record['attributes'])

edge.attributes.pop('uuid', None)
edge.attributes.pop('source_node_uuid', None)
edge.attributes.pop('fact', None)
edge.attributes.pop('name', None)
# ... fact_embedding, group_id, episodes, created_at, expired_at, valid_at, invalid_at
```

## Summary

| Phase | Mechanism | Location |
|---|---|---|
| Save | `edge_data.update(self.attributes)` flattens custom attrs to top level | `edges.py:252` |
| Save | `SET r = $edge_data` writes all keys as relationship properties | `edge_db_queries.py:37` |
| Read | `properties(e) AS attributes` returns the full property map | `edges.py:53` |
| Read | `.pop()` the known fields so only custom attrs remain | `edges.py:469-479` |

The design avoids hardcoding attribute names anywhere: custom attributes ride along as ordinary graph properties, and the round-trip cleanly separates them from the typed schema fields. One note — the bulk path (`ENTITY_EDGE_SAVE_BULK`, `edge_db_queries.py:41-49`) relies on the same `SET r = edge` flattening, so edges built for bulk save must already have their attributes merged into the edge dict the same way.

facts.evidence: the fixed fields are assembled into `edge_data`
edge_data.update(self.attributes or {})   # line 252 — flattens attrs to top level

facts.fact: Any custom key/value in an edge’s attributes is written as relationship properties when saving
facts.evidence: each custom attribute becomes its own first-class property on the `RELATES_TO` relationship

facts.fact: In the get methods and the full-text, similarity, and search utilities, each Cypher query returns properties(r) AS attributes
get_entity_edge_from_record(record) removes reserved keys from the attributes
facts.evidence: then **pops every known field out of `attributes`** so only the genuinely-custom keys remain

facts.fact: Extra attributes added to an edge are preserved through save and load operations
